main_summary.summary: Peggy Jean Connor, born in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, gives a November 30, 2015 interview with Emilye Crosby at the McCain Library and Archives at the University of Southern Mississippi for the Civil Rights History Project. Connor describes a ten-year effort to register to vote that culminated in January 1964, twelve days before Freedom Day. She collected poll taxes for beauty-shop customers, attended a non-violence workshop at the Dorchester Center with Dorothy F. Cotton and Andrew Young, and taught citizenship classes at True Light Church in Hattiesburg. After SNCC's Guyot brought her to a mass meeting where Fannie Lou Hamer spoke, Connor became a Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party organizer and was elected as a delegate to the 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City. She served as MFDP executive secretary and was the named plaintiff in Connor v. Johnson, the redistricting suit that produced Black-majority districts in Jackson. Connor recounts her arrest at a Hattiesburg courthouse march, hosting Rep. John Conyers Jr. at her home during Freedom Day, her daughter Felicia integrating Jefferson Davis Elementary School under freedom-of-choice, the Vernon Dahmer Sr. firebombing aftermath, and the lawsuit she filed against a Hattiesburg drugstore owner who refused to serve her in July 1964.
key_themes: Hattiesburg COFO office and citizenship classes
main_summary.historical_significance:
Connor is the named plaintiff in Connor v. Johnson, the Mississippi redistricting suit that produced majority-Black legislative districts in Jackson, and she served as executive secretary of the MFDP, making her testimony a primary source for the institutional mechanics of the party (record-keeping, board meetings at Rev. R.L.T. Smith's house in Jackson, candidate-litigation strategy with New York attorneys). Her account of the 1964 Atlantic City credentials challenge documents the gender split she observed inside the MFDP delegation over the two-seat compromise, men inclined to accept, women including Fannie Lou Hamer refusing.

Her ground-level recollections add Hattiesburg-specific texture to multiple canonical Movement events: the COFO building burning (after the Movement), the Dahmer firebombing on January 10, 1966 (her recollection of visiting Dahmer at the hospital before his death, and her belief that the NAACP had previously expelled Dahmer for working with SNCC), the Freedom Day march, hosting Rep. John Conyers Jr. during Freedom Day, and her daughter Felicia's freedom-of-choice integration of Jefferson Davis Elementary School. The interview also documents Hattiesburg's local KKK-customer-clientele intelligence network from a Black beautician's vantage point.

chapters.title: Hattiesburg Childhood and Citizenship Class
chapters.summary: Connor describes growing up in Hattiesburg, delivered by Dr. Charles Smith. Her seventh-grade teacher Ruby M. Tatum taught a first-semester citizenship class that emphasized registering to vote at twenty-one. Her father worked at a lumber yard and later as a chauffeur and washed windows downtown; her mother was a housewife.

chapters.title: Beauty Shop and Family Trade
chapters.summary: Connor began shampooing hair at her aunt's beauty shop at age eleven for ten cents per shampoo. She graduated from her aunt's Franklin Beauty School in 1946 and inherited the shop when her aunt left Hattiesburg. The trade ran through her extended family, including a beauty-school operation in Houston, Texas.
related_events: Black-owned beauty-shop economy in mid-twentieth-century Mississippi

chapters.title: Ten Years to Register and Poll-Tax Collection
chapters.summary: Connor describes a ten-year effort to register to vote that succeeded only after a federal court order forced Theron Lynd. She paid the two-year poll tax and collected poll taxes for her beauty-shop customers, walking lists into the circuit clerk's office. She finally received her registration letter in January 1964, twelve days before Freedom Day.
related_events: Federal court order against Theron Lynd

chapters.title: Family, Marriage, and Daughter's Polio
chapters.summary: Connor recounts her brief marriage to an alcoholic veteran who returned changed from the Korean War, her family's separation, and her second daughter's polio illness. The child required iron-lung treatment in Big Spring, Texas, and later Laurel, Mississippi. The daughter died around 1953 from brain aneurysms secondary to polio. Connor moved back with her parents, whose father became a primary caregiver to her surviving children.

chapters.title: Entering the Movement Through COFO and SNCC
chapters.summary: The COFO office across from Connor's beauty shop drew her attention. Guyot crossed the street and invited her to a mass meeting where Fannie Lou Hamer spoke. Victoria Gray Adams then recruited her to a non-violence workshop at the Dorchester Center taught by Dorothy F. Cotton and Andrew Young. Returning to Hattiesburg, Connor approached True Light Church and began teaching citizenship classes after Wednesday-night prayer meetings.

chapters.title: NAACP and Son's Arrest
chapters.summary: Connor joined the NAACP partly as insurance, when a Hattiesburg shoeshine-stand altercation led to her son's arrest, an NAACP card was required for help. Two policemen contradicted each other in court, and the judge ordered the father to take the boy out of Hattiesburg. Her son was hidden in a car trunk to Louisiana and lived in California until the 1970s.
related_events: Targeted policing of Black families in Hattiesburg

chapters.title: Freedom Day and Hosting Rep. Conyers
chapters.summary: Connor recalls Freedom Day in Hattiesburg, January 1964, when ministers and out-of-state workers came down in the pouring rain. Rep. John Conyers Jr. stayed at her house with FBI agents posted for security. Her father took night shifts at the door with a shotgun across his lap and a cane.

chapters.title: Daughter Felicia Integrates Jefferson Davis Elementary
chapters.summary: Under freedom-of-choice, Connor enrolled her daughter Felicia at Jefferson Davis Elementary School on Seventh Street and Hardy. Felicia was the only Black child in her class; teachers held her at the door until all white children were seated. Connor describes the registrar asking which school and her response that it was "the one across from Hercules."

chapters.title: Courthouse March and Jail Time
chapters.summary: After Freedom Day, Connor joined a courthouse picket that the chief officer halted, ordering marchers back to the jail. About sixty were arrested that morning; the count grew to roughly one hundred. Connor was moved to a city farm to keep her from organizing other prisoners. They sang songs, told stories, and drank from a dipper between cells. Released after about a week when a Hattiesburg couple posted their house as $1,000 bond.

chapters.title: Organizing the MFDP and Atlantic City
chapters.summary: Connor was elected as a delegate of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party to the 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City. Three buses brought the delegation. The party had registered better than 3,000 members. Connor describes Lyndon Johnson stopping the MFDP through the two-seat compromise, the men in the delegation inclined to accept on Adam Clayton Powell Jr.'s advice, and the women, led by Fannie Lou Hamer, refusing.

chapters.title: Vernon Dahmer Firebombing and NAACP Tensions
chapters.summary: Connor recalls Vernon Dahmer Sr.'s firebombing on January 10, 1966, visiting him in the hospital while he was still talking about trying to escape the burning house, learning of his death about thirty minutes after leaving. She states the NAACP had previously expelled Dahmer for working with SNCC. She describes the still-smoking house the morning after, and the rumors that some white Hattiesburg residents discreetly aided Movement workers, including a William Carey College professor.

chapters.title: Head Start, CDGM, and the Drugstore Suit
chapters.summary: Connor describes her work as a system community worker on the Child Development Group of Mississippi (CDGM) Head Start program, the first such program in the United States, originally explained to MFDP delegates by Sargent Shriver in Washington. She was later moved out of an executive role, she suspects, for political activity. Connor also recounts her 1964 sit-in attempt at a Hattiesburg drugstore lunch counter and the four-year lawsuit she filed against the owner.

chapters.title: Connor v. Johnson and Movement Reflections
chapters.summary: Connor explains that as MFDP executive secretary she became the named plaintiff in Connor v. Johnson, the suit that redrew Mississippi legislative district lines and produced Black-majority districts in Jackson. She describes board meetings at Rev. R.L.T. Smith's house in Jackson with New York lawyers in khaki pants. She reflects on present-day Black elected officials, the 2011 oral history that finally drew her out of shyness, and Southern Mississippi's transformation since the 1960s.

loc_video.caption: Peggy Jean Connor oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, 2015 November 30
